name: Avenc de Son Nassi
starting_point: Restaurant Can Penaso on the Ma-11 road from Palma to Sóller. Park at the restaurant, walk along the road towards Sóller to km 15.800, then leave the road to enter the s'Alqueria estate (permission required).
equipment: Vertical progression equipment plus one rope of approximately 25 m.
description: A 14-meter deep pit on the slopes of Puig de Son Nassi with two entrances connected by a cracked rock bridge threatening collapse. Inside, a remarkable fig tree has grown from a seed germinating 4-5 meters above the floor, extending its roots down to reach the ground with an unusually large trunk. The cave walls are well-decorated with speleothems, and the pit drains water through narrow openings. Designated as a site of special speleological interest.
full_description:
When on November 24, 2005, while hiking, it occurred to me to climb to the summit of the Puig de Son Nassi accompanied by my wife, we passed by the pit in question, but it never crossed my mind that I would end up descending into its interior to explore it and help with its survey, since at that time I had not yet made the decision to practice speleology. Finally, two years later, accompanied by J. A. Encinas on a rainy day, we decided to venture into its depths without knowing if that pit I had found by chance was really anything more than a simple hole. It was interesting to discover, leaning over one of its two openings, the bottom of the pit, giving us already from above the impression that it had been worth climbing up to it, and even more so when, to our surprise, we found another one just a few meters away.

Since the pit is situated very close to the saddle separating the Puig de s'Alqueria from the Puig de Son Nassi, it is possible that its formation began with a tearing of the latter mountain due to the pressure exerted by the weight of the Puig de s'Alqueria upon it; the water surely did the rest.

Once again the fig tree is omnipresent in a pit, being in this case one of the most beautiful, if not the most beautiful, since the seed germinated just about 4 or 5 meters from the bottom on a rock, forcing the tree to extend its roots over the rock until finding the floor of the pit, while at the same time growing and thickening until reaching a trunk of considerable dimensions never seen by me inside a cavity.

A holm oak, if I remember correctly, will serve us to secure the rope and begin the descent into the interior of the pit, at one point passing our body between the wall and the trunk of the fig tree, so one must be careful to pass the rope through that spot beforehand. Once at the bottom, one can appreciate the dimensions of the cavity, which at this point are approximately seven meters and thirty centimeters long by about three meters wide at its widest part.

Looking toward the exterior, we can also observe how the two entrance openings of this pit are separated by a kind of bridge, with the connecting rock cracked and threatening to break at any moment. We can also see that the walls of the pit, except for the side where the fig tree has grown, are well decorated with concretions, giving the pit a cozy appearance despite, as I said before, being threatened by the possible collapse of the rock that separates the two entrances.

Once the exploration of the pit has begun, one can quickly verify that at one of the two ends and in each of the two corners, the pit has continuity, with water draining through them from the slopes of the Puig de Son Nassi. Through the crack on the left side, we quickly realize that besides being inaccessible to us, if we throw a stone into it, the depth is small, although one can never be sure of that since large blocks of stone have fallen from the outside at this location, probably causing its obstruction. In the other corner on the same side, however, the opening that provides continuity to the pit is of larger dimensions, inviting exploration, since we have also been able to verify that at least five or six meters vertically it extends further. The problem is that after trying, we were unable to access its interior to continue the exploration, as the slimmest among us, after trying insistently, had to give up.

The Avenc de Son Nassi is considered of special speleological interest.

Near this pit is the Avenc des Coll de Son Nassi, which we can visit to complete our stay in the area.
